title: CMAI Applauds New CCTS Guidelines: Accelerating the Path to National Compliance Market
excerpt: The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) recently released updated guidelines for India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme. CMAI analysis highlights the transformative potential for Indian industries.
body:
The government of India, through the Ministry of Power and Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), has released the implementation blueprint for the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS). This initiative lays the regulatory foundation for a compliance-based domestic carbon market, driving decarbonization across carbon-intensive sectors such as steel, cement, and thermal power.

As the leading industry body, the Carbon Markets Association of India (CMAI) has worked closely with policy circles to represent stakeholder interests. The new rules specify greenhouse gas emission intensity targets for obligated entities. Those who overachieve their targets will receive Carbon Credit Certificates (CCCs), which can be sold on national registries.

Our Executive Director noted, 'The transition from PAT (Perform, Achieve and Trade) to CCTS is a landmark step. It aligns Indian corporate climate actions with international standards, while preparing local markets for cross-border carbon mechanisms.'
author: CMAI Policy Desk

title: Scaling Biochar in Madhya Pradesh: CRIA Launches Farmer-Centric Carbon Dioxide Removal Program
excerpt: Carbon Removal India Alliance (CRIA) announces a major collaboration with agricultural collectives to deploy pyrolyzers, generating high-durability carbon credits.
body:
In a bid to demonstrate the agricultural and climate benefits of biochar, the Carbon Removal India Alliance (CRIA) has kicked off a regional scaling campaign in Madhya Pradesh. In partnership with three Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs), the initiative will distribute 150 low-emission pyrolysis units.

Biochar offers a double-win for the Indian context: it sequesters carbon in soils for hundreds of years while enhancing water retention and crop yields. By utilizing agricultural residues that would otherwise be burned (contributing to air pollution), the project will generate high-quality carbon removal certificates.

'This program addresses one of India's biggest seasonal challenges - crop stubble burning - while generating additional revenue streams for farmers through global carbon markets,' said a senior CRIA analyst. The program aims to remove 15,000 tons of CO2 equivalent annually by 2028.
author: CRIA Science Committee

title: Article 6.2 and India: Navigating the New Era of Bilateral Carbon Transfers
excerpt: With India finalizing its list of eligible activities for international transfer under Article 6.2, we examine how developers can capitalize on these cross-border opportunities.
body:
Under the Paris Agreement, Article 6.2 allows countries to trade emission reductions (ITMOs) through bilateral agreements. The Government of India recently designated key areas for Article 6.2, including green hydrogen, solar thermal energy, and high-efficiency cookstoves.

CMAI has hosted a series of roundtables with the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) to clarify the processes for corresponding adjustments. Ensuring no double-counting and maintaining rigorous national registries will be critical to protecting local carbon stocks while attracting foreign climate investment.

Developers will need to satisfy both host-country rules and purchasing-country requirements. This article maps out the certification workflow, the risk parameters of Corresponding Adjustments, and the forecast for credit pricing.
author: Advisory Team

title: Enhanced Rock Weathering Field Trials Show High Carbon Sequestration Potential in Deccan Trap Basalts
excerpt: A joint study by CRIA and leading geochemists highlights the use of basalt dust on agricultural fields as a viable, scalable carbon dioxide removal (CDR) method.
body:
Enhanced Rock Weathering (ERW) is emerging as a front-runner for large-scale carbon removal. By grinding silicate rocks like basalt and spreading them on cropland, the natural weathering process is accelerated, drawing down CO2 from the atmosphere and converting it into stable bicarbonate in groundwater.

CRIA's research team has completed the first phase of field trials across 500 hectares of cropland in Maharashtra. The region’s Deccan Trap basalt is mineralogically ideal for ERW. Initial data shows significant increase in soil pH, magnesium retention, and high carbon sequestration rates per ton of basalt applied.

'ERW represents a major opportunity for India because of our vast basalt reserves and extensive agricultural lands. It's a stable, gigaton-scale pathway that could positioning India as a global exporter of high-permanence credits,' remarked the lead scientist.
author: Dr. A. K. Sen

title: Green Credit Programme (GCP): A New Paradigm for Corporate Sustainability in India
excerpt: India's Green Credit Programme introduces incentives for water conservation and afforestation. CMAI analyzes its integration with the national carbon market.
body:
The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) has introduced the Green Credit Programme (GCP). Unlike traditional carbon credits which measure greenhouse gas mitigation, green credits will incentivize a broader spectrum of environmental actions, including water conservation, sustainable agriculture, waste management, and mangrove restoration.

CMAI's latest brief evaluates the potential synergies and overlaps between CCTS (carbon credits) and GCP (green credits). There is immense interest among Indian corporations in leveraging both instruments. However, establishing distinct methodologies and avoiding double-claiming of benefits remains a primary administrative challenge.

This article outlines the registry status, methods for measuring credits, and the pilot projects being initiated under the tree plantation methodologies.
author: CMAI Policy Desk
